segments.s106-g1: وزانجا is an adverbial phrasing 'and/from there' placed before the motion phrase; سوی + noun is a directional prepositional phrase ('toward Pars'). اندر is a locative/preverb meaning 'into/within' that precedes the past-tense verb کشید (here a past action 'moved/drew'), so the whole chunk means 'from there he moved toward Pars.'
segments.s106-g2: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). در پارس is locative 'in Pars.' بد qualifies گنجها as 'ill-/bad-' or 'ill-omened' treasures; را is the definite object/topic marker attached to گنجها; کلید is a noun 'key' used figuratively ('the key to the treasures'). Together: 'for in Pars was the key of the ill-omened treasures.'
segments.s106-g3: نشستنگه is a noun meaning 'seat/seat-place' (the place of sitting). آن گه is a temporal phrase 'then/at that time.' به اسطخر is the locative 'at Estakhr.' بود is the past copula 'was.' Word order is topic/time + locative + copula: 'His seat then was at Estakhr.'
segments.s106-g4: کیان را marks the Kians as the topical/affected party (را = topical/definite marker). The sequence ب + دان here functions as an older form 'به آن' meaning 'in/at that' (the segmentation in the lexicon is not literal; grammatically it's the preposition 'به' + demonstrative 'آن'). جایگه is 'place,' فخر is 'pride,' and بود is the copula 'was.' So: 'The Kians had pride in that place.'
segments.s106-g5: جهانی here denotes 'the world'/'all people' (used as a collective noun). سوی او is the directional phrase 'toward him.' نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'they set/placed' and combines with روی ('face') to form the idiom 'نهادند روی' = 'they turned their faces.' The whole means 'the world turned its face to him.'
segments.s106-g6: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause. او بود is the copula 'he was.' سالار is a title 'chief/lord.' دیهیم جوی is a compound where دیهیم relates to village/rural folk and جوی (from جستن) means 'seeking/searching' or 'seeker'; together they describe the kind of lord he was ('a lord who sought or cared for the rural folk'). The phrase functions as the predicate: 'for he was the lord (سالار) ...'.
segments.s106-g7: به تخت کیان is the direction/target phrase 'on/onto the Kians' throne.' اندر (here) reinforces motion 'into/on.' آورد is the past tense 'brought/placed' and پای 'foot' is the object: an idiom meaning 'he set his foot on the throne' (i.e., took the throne).
segments.s106-g8: به داد و به آیین is a paired prepositional phrase 'with justice and with custom/according to the ritual.' داد here means 'justice' (not the past of 'to give'), و is the coordinating 'and,' آیین is 'custom/ritual,' and فرخنده‌رای is an adjective 'auspicious/propitious' qualifying آیین. The phrase describes how he assumed/held the throne: 'in justice and in an auspicious practice.'
segments.s106-g25: چراگاهشان is چراگاه (grazing place, pasture) + possessive suffix -شان “their”. بارگاه is a lofty noun ‘court/palace’ functioning as the predicate noun. منست is an archaic/poetic copular/linked form (= من است or a contracted predicate) that links بارگاه to the speaker (‘is mine’/‘is my…’). Word order is noun + predicate, typical of Persian verse.
segments.s106-g26: هرآنکس که = ‘whoever that…’ (هرآنکس = ‘whoever’; که = relative particle ‘who/that’). اندر سپاه = 'in/within the army/host' (اندر is a poetic preposition). منست again closes the clause as a predicate (archaic/poetic linking form); here it predicates membership/status with respect to the speaker’s host.
segments.s106-g27: وزان رفته = وز آن رفته, ‘and from/of those gone/departed’ (وزان is a fused poetic form meaning ‘and from that/and of those’). نام‌آوران is the plural ‘the renowned/illustrious ones’. یاد کرد is a light-verb construction: یاد (remembrance) + کرد (did/made) = ‘he commemorated/remembered’. Word order is topical/poetic: source/topic → the people → the verbal phrase.
segments.s106-g28: به داد و دهش گیتی آباد کرد is an instrumental/result clause: به + means (داد ‘justice’ و دهش ‘giving/generosity’) + گیتی آباد کرد ‘he made the world prosperous’. Here به functions as ‘by/with’ (means), داد = ‘justice’, دهش = ‘the act of giving/giving’, گیتی = ‘the world’, آباد = predicate adjective ‘prosperous/settled’, and کرد is the past verb ‘made/did’. The coordinating و links two instruments.
segments.s106-g29: برین گونه = بر + این (here orthographically split as بر + ین) + گونه = ‘in this manner/so’; this is a fixed phrase meaning ‘thus’ or ‘in this way’. صدسال شادان بزیست is a temporal phrase + predicate: صدسال ‘a hundred years’ (duration) + شادان ‘joyful’ (adverbial/attributive) + بزیست (past tense, 3rd sg. ‘he/they lived’ in older/formal diction). The verb bazīst is older/poetic for ‘lived’. Word order places the manner/time before the verb, typical in poetry.
segments.s106-g30: نگر (imperative) = ‘look!’ or ‘see!’. تا connects the imperative to the content that follows and here functions as ‘that/so that’ introducing the purpose/content of the look: تا چنین در جهان شاه کیست = ‘to see who in the world is such a king’. چنین = ‘such/so’; در جهان = ‘in the world’; شاه = ‘king’; کی (archaic interrogative) = ‘who’; ست = poetic/copular ‘is’ (short form of است). The clause is an imperative + subordinate content clause.
segments.s106-g31: This is compact, archaic word order. پسر بد = ‘the bad/wicked son’. مر here is an old/poetic form meaning ‘my/to me’ (possessive/benefactive), so پسر بد مر = ‘my wicked son’. او را is the object marker + pronoun ‘him’. خردمند چار = ‘a wise remedy’ (خردمند ‘wise’ modifying چار ‘remedy’). The whole segment is an imperative/requestal sense: ‘make my wicked son into a wise remedy’ (چار as ‘remedy’ rather than the numeral).
segments.s106-g32: که بودند زو در جهان یادگار is a purpose/result clause introduced by که = ‘that/so that’. بودند is the past plural form of بودن ‘to be’ (here marking an optative/result: ‘there might be/there were’). ز = poetic preposition ‘from/of’, و = enclitic pronoun referring back to the son (‘of him’), در جهان = ‘in the world’, یادگار = ‘a memorial/memento’. Together: ‘so that from him there might be a memorial in the world.’
segments.s106-g9: چنین is an adverb fronting the clause meaning 'thus/so.' گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (narrative perfect), introducing speech. با + نامور مهتران marks the addressees: با here 'to/with' (addressing a group) and نامور مهتران = 'the renowned/celebrated elders or chiefs' (مهتران = plural of a poetic/archaic noun meaning senior leaders).
segments.s106-g10: که introduces the reported content ('that'). گیتی is the subject 'the world.' مرا here is the combination of the enclitic possessive -م and the object marker را (rendered in the word list as separate tokens 'م' and 'را'); together they indicate possession/experiencer ('is mine' / 'to me'). از... تا... is the standard 'from... to...' range phrase, with کران 'edge/limit' repeated for 'from edge to edge.'
segments.s106-g11: اگر begins a conditional clause ('if'). پیل is the subject 'elephant' (here probably figurative for something large). با + پشه means 'together with/alongside a gnat' (contrast of large and small). کین is the object 'hatred/vengeance.' آورد is the past tense verb 'brought/caused' (آوردن) used here to mean 'caused' or 'brought about.'
segments.s106-g12: همه 'all/every' modifies رخنه 'breach; opening.' در here is the locative/instrumental preposition 'in/into.' داد is the past form of دادن 'gave' (here best read as part of a verbal action 'made/gave' a breach). و links the two coordinated verb phrases; دین 'religion/law' is the object of the second آورد 'brought' (i.e., 'brought religion' or 'brought about law/order'). The clause describes consequences: causing breaches and bringing (a) religion/ordinance.
segments.s106-g13: نخواهم is the negated present/future first-person 'I do not want / I will not want' (negation + personal ending). به گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'in/for the world.' جز از راستی is an exception phrase 'except from truth' = 'except righteousness' (جز ... از = 'apart from, except').
segments.s106-g14: که here gives the reason ('for/because'): 'for God's anger...'. خشم خدا is the noun phrase 'God's anger.' آورد is the past tense 'has brought/caused' (آوردن) and کاستی is the result 'deficiency/decline/diminution.' So the clause means 'because God's wrath has brought decline.'
segments.s106-g15: تن آسانی is a verbless nominal clause where 'تن' (body/self) and 'آسانی' (ease/comfort) are linked; read as 'the body's ease' or 'comfort for the body.' از introduces the source 'from/out of' and درد و رنج is the coordinated noun phrase 'pain and suffering.' منست is a poetic/archaic copular phrase meaning 'is mine' / 'belongs to me' (here serving as the predicate: '... is mine' or 'is due to me').
segments.s106-g16: کجا here functions as 'where/wherever.' خاک و آبست likely scans as 'there is earth and water' (in the manuscript the verb can appear contracted: خاک و آب است), with گنج 'treasure' as the subject of the following predicate. منست again is the poetic copula meaning 'is mine' or 'is (for) me' — so 'where there is earth and water, my treasure is (there).'
segments.s106-g49: تو‌گر is the conditional particle 'if (you)'; دادگر باشی is a present-subjunctive/declarative copular construction meaning 'you are / you be just' (باشی is the subjunctive/present form of بودن used in conditions). پاک دین is an adjectival phrase 'pure in religion/devout' coordinated by و with دادگر; there is no repeated copula so both adjectives apply to the same implied subject 'you'.
segments.s106-g50: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' (like از); هر کس means 'every/each person' and is the reference for what you 'receive/find'. نیابی is the negative present/subjunctive form of یابیدن, meaning 'you will not find/obtain'; the phrase به جز آفرین ('except praise') closes the clause — the whole means you will get nothing from anyone but praise.
segments.s106-g51: وگر is the conditional particle 'and/but if'; آز is the noun 'greed/avarice' serving as grammatical subject; گیرد is third-person singular present/subjunctive of گرفتن 'seizes/takes'; سرت را is the definite object 'your head' marked by the object marker را; به دام is a prepositional phrase 'into a trap/into entrapment' describing how/where the seizure takes place.
segments.s106-g52: برآری is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'you will lift/bring forth/draw' (here understood as drawing a sword); یکی تیغ تیز is the direct object 'a sharp sword' (یکی = one/a, تیغ = sword, تیز = sharp); از نیام is the source phrase 'out of the scabbard' (از = from/out of, نیام = scabbard). Word order is verb + object + source, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s106-g53: بگفت is simple past 'he said'; این refers to the foregoing remark 'this'; و شد introduces the result/sequence 'and then it came to pass / he departed' — the phrase زین جهان فراخ is poetic and functions as 'from this wide/spacious world', i.e. 'he departed this world' (an idiomatic way to say he died or left).
segments.s106-g54: گزین کرد is a two-word past-tense verb 'he chose/did choose' (گزین = choose, کرد = made/did); صندوق is the chosen object 'a chest'; بر جای کاخ is a locative phrase 'on/in the place of the palace' or more naturally 'in the palace' (بر = on/upon, جای = place, کاخ = palace); the verb governs the direct object صندوق and the following locative.
segments.s106-g55: بسر شد is an idiom meaning 'came to an end' or 'was brought to a close' (بسر in this construction expresses 'to completion' and شد is the past auxiliary/verb); کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb; قصه کیقباد is the noun phrase 'the story/tale of Kay Qobad' (noun + proper name in apposition/genitive sense).
segments.s106-g56: ز here means 'about/of' (poetic use of از), کاووس is the name 'Kavus'; باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary'; سخن کرد is the verbal phrase 'to speak/tell' (سخن = speech/tale, کرد = do/say used here as main verb); یاد at the end means 'remembrance/memory' and the whole inverted order gives 'one must speak (in) the remembrance of Kavus' — i.e. we must tell/recall Kavus.
segments.s106-g41: سر ماه is a temporal expression 'at the head of the month' = 'at the beginning of the month.' کاووس is the subject (the king). کی را is the interrogative object 'whom' with the object marker را immediately after it. بخواند is third-person singular (narrative) verb 'called/summoned' (here used to report that Kavus summoned someone).
segments.s106-g42: ز داد و دهش is an ablative/causal phrase 'by/from his justice and generosity'; چند is the interrogative 'how many' modifying an implied group of people; با او براند literally 'he led/drave with him' (براند, 3rd-person verb of راندن, here 'sent/led [people] with him'). The overall phrasing asks how many people he sent along by means of his justice and gifts.
segments.s106-g43: بدو is a dative clitic phrase 'to him.' گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'he said.' ما بر نهادیم رخت is a fixed verbal expression: بر + نهادیم (we placed/put) with رخت 'baggage/possessions' — together meaning 'we put/packed our baggage' or 'we have prepared to depart.'
segments.s106-g44: تو is the vocative/imperative 'you.' بسپار is an imperative/infinitival sense 'entrust/leave (to),' here a command 'entrust (it).' تابوت 'coffin' is the direct object; و links the two commands; بردار is an imperative 'take up!' or 'lift!'; تخت 'throne' is the object of بردار. The line gives two short commands addressed to 'you.'
segments.s106-g45: چنانم is 'I am such' (چنان + -م 'I'). که introduces a clause 'that' and گویی is a parenthetical 'as if/one would say' (idiomatic 'it seems'). ز البرز کوه is an ablative phrase 'from Alborz mountain' (ز + proper name + noun), marking origin/source.
segments.s106-g46: کنون آمدم is the time adverb 'now' plus past first-person آمدم 'I came.' شادمان is a predicate adjective 'joyful/rejoicing' describing the speaker; با گروه 'with a company' is a prepositional phrase indicating accompaniment. Word order places manner/accompaniment after the verb.
segments.s106-g47: چو is the comparative/simile 'like/as.' بختی is 'a fortune / chance' (here the subject of the simile). که introduces the relative clause 'that,' بی‌آگهی 'unaware/unknowingly' modifies the verb, and بگذرد (3rd-person singular) means 'passes' — together 'like a fortune that passes without notice.'
segments.s106-g48: پرستنده او is the subject phrase 'its worshipper' (پرستنده 'worshipper' + object pronoun او 'it/him'); ندارد is the negative predicate 'does not have'; خرد 'wisdom' is the direct object. The clause states that anyone who worships that (fortune) lacks wisdom.
segments.s106-g17: سپاهی و شهری همه یکسرند = subject phrase + predicate. سپاهی (a collective noun) and شهری (townspeople/city-dwellers) are joined by و. همه is an inclusive subject marker 'all/everyone', and یکسرند is a poetic predicate meaning 'are wholly/one and the same' (an archaic/poetic copula-like predicate here; -ند shows plural). Word order: subject(s) — adverbial 'همه' — predicate.
segments.s106-g18: همه پادشاهی مرا لشکرند = 'all the pādshāhī (kings/kingdoms) are an army for me'. همه paves the noun as 'all', پادشاهی is a collective (here meaning the kings/kingdoms), مرا (م + را) marks the beneficiary/possessor 'for me / my', and لشکرند is the predicate 'are an army' (لشکر 'army' used predicatively; -ند plural ending). The sequence is subject — indirect object/beneficiary — predicate.
segments.s106-g19: همه در پناه جهاندار بید = 'All are in the refuge/protection of the world-ruler.' در + پناه is the prepositional phrase 'in the protection'; جهاندار is an epithet for the sovereign ('world-holder'/'lord of the world'); بید is an archaic poetic form of the copula 'are' (not the modern words listed in the senses), so it functions as the verb 'are'.
segments.s106-g20: خردمند بید و بی‌آزار بید = two adjectival predicates joined by و. خردمند 'wise' and بی‌آزار 'harmless, inoffensive' are predicative adjectives; each is followed by the archaic copula بید (here 'are'), giving a balanced, parallel structure.
segments.s106-g21: هر آنکس که دارد خورید و دهید = a relative/hortatory clause. هر آنکس که دارد = 'whoever has' (دارد = present 'has/possesses'); خورید و دهید are verbs in the -ید form that function as hortative/jussive/imperative 'let him eat and let him give' (classical poetic usage often employs -ید for imperative/subjunctive). The clause therefore invites those who have (food) to eat and to give.
segments.s106-g22: سپاسی ز خوردن به من برنهید = main admonition. سپاسی 'a thanks/praise' is the object; ز + خوردن is a genitive/ablative 'from/of the eating' (خوردن as verbal noun); به من 'to/for me' marks the beneficiary; برنهید is an archaic verbal form used here with a prohibitory sense 'do not withhold/deny me' (classical usages can differ). The clause therefore says 'do not withhold thanks to me for the eating.'
segments.s106-g23: هرآنکس کجا بازماند ز خورد = 'Whoever somewhere/anywhere remains left from eating' — i.e. 'whoever is left without food.' هرآنکس 'whoever'; کجا here 'where/anywhere' in a negative/indefinite sense; بازماند (present) 'remains/is left' (passive/inasmuch as he is left behind); ز خورد literally 'from eating' = 'without food' (classical contraction of از + خورد).
segments.s106-g24: ندارد همی توشه کارکرد = 'has not even provisions for action/for the undertaking.' ندارد = 'does not have' (negated present of داشتن); همی is an emphatic/limiting particle (here 'even/indeed'); توشه 'provisions/supplies'; کارکرد is a verbal-noun sense 'the doing/undertaking' or 'work' — together 'provisions for the task/journey/effort.' Word order places the negated verb before its objects (classical Persian style).
segments.s106-g33: نخستین is a nominal head meaning “the first.” چو here is the comparative/temporal particle “like / as,” introducing a likeness; it links to the following name. کاووس باآفرین is a name plus an epithet: کاووس (Kavus) + باآفرین (literally “with‑praise,” an honorific epithet ‘the Praiseworthy’). The clause is verbless (typical in lists) and functions as an appositive or qualifier to ‘the first.’
segments.s106-g34: This small clause supplies identities without an explicit copula (بود is omitted). کی is the classical interrogative/relational “who/which” used here to mark identification (‘who was…’). آرش is the proper name. و coordinates the next phrase; دگر means “another/the other.” The repeated کی introduces the second identification (کی پشین = ‘who was Peshin’). The copula is implied, a common archaic/poetic economy.
segments.s106-g35: چهارم is ‘the fourth.’ The following کجا here is used in a poetic/questioning sense (“what/where” about a name) and is followed by آرشش (آرش with the possessive suffix -ش ‘his Arash’ or ‘Arash of him’), then بود (past copula ‘was’) and نام ‘name’. The word order is inverted and elliptical: the speaker asks (or introduces) the question of the fourth’s name; this is typical compressed, poetic syntax.
segments.s106-g36: سپردند is a past transitive verb in the third‑person plural ‘they entrusted/handed over’ (subject omitted but understood). گیتی is the direct object ‘the world.’ به introduces the indirect object or beneficiary; آرام و کام here function as coordinated nouns (likely personal names in context, though lexically they also mean ‘calm’ and ‘desire’). The whole clause states that the world was handed over to ‘Aram and Kam.’
segments.s106-g37: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ صد سال بگذشت is a temporal clause in past tense: ‘a hundred years passed.’ با introduces accompaniment or possession here — با تاج و تخت ‘with crown and throne’ — indicating that those years were spent with royal dignity (crown and throne).
segments.s106-g38: سرانجام is an adverb ‘finally’ or ‘in the end.’ تاب is a poetic noun meaning ‘splendor/brightness’ here. اندر آمد is a compound verbal phrase ‘came into / came upon’ (اندر = in/into + آمد = came). به بخت ‘to fate/fortune’ indicates the beneficiary; the line therefore says that at last brightness/splendor came to his fortune. Word order (noun + اندر آمد + به + بخت) is a poetic inversion.
segments.s106-g39: چو is ‘when.’ دانست is the past tense ‘he knew.’ کامد is an older/formal verb meaning ‘comes’ or ‘arrives’ (here part of the clause ‘[that] comes’), so دانست + کامد forms a reported clause ‘he knew that … was coming.’ به نزدیک مرگ is the phrase ‘near to death / death approaching.’ Altogether: ‘when he knew that death was approaching.’
segments.s106-g40: This clause gives the result: بپژمرد is the infinitive/ subjunctive form ‘to wither’ (here functioning as a predicative verb). خواهد is the auxiliary/future-marker ‘will’ (in classical style it often follows the main verb: بپژمرد خواهد). همی is an emphatic/continuous particle ‘still/ever’ used here for aspect. سبز برگ is the object ‘green leaf.’ The line therefore reads idiomatically as a semi‑prophetic past/future: ‘the green leaf would wither.’

sentences.s106-s1: From there he moved toward Pars, for in Pars he had the key to the ill-omened treasures.
sentences.s106-s2: His seat then was at Estakhr; the Kians took pride in that place.
sentences.s106-s3: All the world turned its face toward him, for he was the lord who sought the rural folk.
sentences.s106-s4: He set his foot upon the Kians' throne, in justice and according to an auspicious custom.
sentences.s106-s13: Their grazing-grounds are my palace; whoever is in my host (army) is so.
sentences.s106-s14: And of those departed famed ones he made remembrance; by justice and giving he made the world prosper.
sentences.s106-s15: Thus, they lived a hundred years joyfully; look and see who in the world is such a king.
sentences.s106-s16: Make my wicked son a wise remedy, that there might be from him a memorial in the world.
sentences.s106-s5: Thus he said to the renowned chiefs: 'The world is mine from edge to edge.'
sentences.s106-s6: If an elephant together with a gnat brought hatred, they caused every breach and brought (forth) religion.
sentences.s106-s7: I want nothing in the world except truth/righteousness, for God's anger has brought decline.
sentences.s106-s8: The body's ease comes out of my pain and suffering; wherever there is earth and water, there is my treasure.
sentences.s106-s25: If you are just and devout, you will find from everyone nothing except praise.
sentences.s106-s26: But if greed seizes you, it will ensnare your head; you will draw a sharp sword from the scabbard.
sentences.s106-s27: He said this and then left this spacious world; he chose a chest and placed it in (or upon) the palace.
sentences.s106-s28: Now the tale of Kay Qobad has come to an end; it is necessary to speak of Kavus.
sentences.s106-s21: At the beginning of the month, whom did Kavus call, and with how many (people) did he send (them) with his justice and gifts?
sentences.s106-s22: He said to him, 'We have packed our baggage; you, commit the coffin and lift the throne.'
sentences.s106-s23: I am such that, as if from Mount Alborz, I have now come rejoicing with a company.
sentences.s106-s24: Like a chance fortune that passes unknowingly, its worshipper has no wisdom.
sentences.s106-s9: Soldiers and townspeople alike are all of one piece; all the kings (kingdoms) are my army.
sentences.s106-s10: All are in the protection of the sovereign of the world; they are wise and harmless.
sentences.s106-s11: Whoever has (food), let him eat and let him give; with regard to the eating, do not withhold thanks from me.
sentences.s106-s12: Whoever anywhere is left without food has no provision for his undertaking.
sentences.s106-s17: The first was Arash — like Kavus the Praiseworthy; the second, another, was Peshin.
sentences.s106-s18: As for the fourth — what was his name? — they entrusted the world to Aram and Kam.
sentences.s106-s19: When a hundred years had passed with crown and throne, at last the splendor came to (his) fortune.
sentences.s106-s20: When he knew that death was drawing near, the green leaf would wither.
